metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify where and how the `fields` dict (title, description, priority, status, assignee, labels) is read from the local ticket store and injected into the follow-on dict before `create_one` is invoked — the existing `action="create_after_hard_delete"` payload has no `fields` key and `create_one` will produce an empty Jira issue without it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The payload carries the `fields` dict create_one needs (title/description/priority/status/assignee, labels) from the preserved local ticket,' but the existing follow-on dict built at apply_inbound.py:553-558 contains ONLY {direction, action, target, local_id} — there is NO `fields` key. create_one (dispatch_one.py:194,227) calls mutation.get('fields', {}) and will receive an empty dict, creating a Jira issue with no title, description, priority, assignee, or status. The plan cannot 'consume the existing follow-on shape as-is' AND have create_one receive the needed fields — those fields must be read from the local ticket store and injected into the follow-on dict before create_one is invoked.
findings.location: What section, item 3 / apply_inbound.py:553-558 / AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: In the consumer branch in applier.py (or at the point where the follow-on is enqueued), read the local ticket's reducer output for `local_id` (e.g. via `pass_io` / the ticket store), build a `fields` dict with title/description/priority/status/assignee/labels, and set `follow_on['fields'] = fields` before passing the dict to `create_one`. Alternatively, update `_apply_inbound_delete`'s hard_delete branch to populate `follow_on['fields']` from the just-preserved local content at emit time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify WHERE and HOW the `fields` dict (title, description, priority, status, assignee, labels) is populated into the `create_after_hard_delete` follow-on — either by reading the preserved local ticket directory inside `_apply_inbound_delete` before emitting the follow-on, or by having the consumer branch in `applier.py` read them from disk; update the follow-on shape accordingly.
findings.finding: The existing follow-on dict emitted by `apply_inbound.py:553-558` contains only `{direction, action, target, local_id}` — no `fields` key. But `create_one` (dispatch_one.py:227) reads `mutation.get('fields', {})` to build the JQL dedup label query AND the `create_issue` call. The plan asserts 'The payload carries the `fields` dict create_one needs' but this is false against the live code: the existing shape is missing `fields`, so the consumer branch as specified would produce an empty-title create or a dedup miss.
findings.location: What section item 2 / What section item 3; apply_inbound.py:553-558; dispatch_one.py:194-241
findings.suggested_fix: In `_apply_inbound_delete`, after determining `local_id`, read the preserved local ticket fields from the tracker directory (e.g., the ticket's TOML/JSON front-matter) and include them as `fields` in the follow-on dict before setting `result_payload['follow_on']`. The consumer branch in applier.py can then pass the follow-on dict directly to `create_one`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit work item specifying that `apply_inbound.py:553-558` must be extended to read the preserved local ticket's fields (title, description, priority, status, assignee, labels) from the tracker directory and include them in the follow-on dict under a `fields` key, so `create_one` has the data it requires.
findings.finding: The plan says to 'consume the EXISTING `action="create_after_hard_delete"` dict' and that 'The payload carries the `fields` dict create_one needs (title/description/priority/status/assignee, labels) from the preserved local ticket.' However, the actual existing follow-on built in `apply_inbound.py:553-558` contains only `direction`, `action`, `target`, and `local_id` — NO `fields` key. `create_one` at `dispatch_one.py:227` does `_raw_fields = mutation.get('fields', {})` and will produce an empty fields dict, causing `create_issue` to raise `ValueError('title/summary is empty')`. The plan never specifies that the follow-on construction must be extended to populate `fields` from the preserved local ticket content.
findings.location: What section, item 2 and item 3; `apply_inbound.py:553-558`
findings.suggested_fix: In the follow-on construction block (apply_inbound.py:541-558), read the local ticket's `events` or a snapshot file to extract the current field values and add `"fields": {"title": ..., "description": ..., ...}` to the follow-on dict before it is stored in `result_payload["follow_on"]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in What #3 (or in a new What sub-item) exactly where the `fields` dict is sourced from (e.g. 'read preserved local ticket YAML/event files from `tracker_dir/local_id/` and map title/description/priority/status/assignee/labels into `follow_on["fields"]` at the point where the follow-on is built in `_apply_inbound_delete`') so an implementer knows what code to write.
findings.finding: The follow-on dict schema (`apply_inbound.py:553-558`) has no `fields` key, yet `create_one` requires `mutation.get('fields', {})` to build the Jira issue. The plan asserts 'The payload carries the `fields` dict create_one needs' but never specifies which code populates `fields` into the follow-on before it reaches `create_one`.
findings.location: What #3 ('The payload carries the `fields` dict create_one needs'); apply_inbound.py:553-558; dispatch_one.py:227

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend Step 3 (and the follow-on shape in apply_inbound.py:553-558) to explicitly include a `fields` dict sourced from the preserved local ticket content (read from the tracker dir using the same helpers already used in `_apply_inbound_create`) before the follow-on is emitted; confirm create_one's contract is satisfied.
findings.finding: The existing follow-on dict built at apply_inbound.py:553-558 contains no `fields` key; the plan instructs consuming it AS-IS via create_one, but create_one requires a populated `fields` dict (title/summary at minimum) or it produces an empty-titled Jira issue / raises ValueError. The plan's claim that 'The payload carries the fields dict create_one needs' is false for the current shape.
findings.location: What / Step 3 ('The payload carries the fields dict create_one needs'); apply_inbound.py:553-558; dispatch_one.py:227-232
findings.suggested_fix: In `_apply_inbound_delete`'s hard_delete branch, read the local ticket's current field values (title, description, priority, status, assignee) from the tracker dir (using `_read_latest_status` / ticket reducer as already done in `_apply_inbound_create`) and include them as `follow_on['fields']`. The plan should explicitly describe this field-population step so the implementer knows it is required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in What item 5 whether the stale-reverse deletion is inside `bind_confirm` (affecting all callers — requiring acknowledgement of other call sites and a rollback path) or is a local cleanup in the new consumer branch only (safe); if the former, add an expand-contract note and identify all call sites.
findings.finding: The plan requires deleting `reverse[old_key]` 'in the same save' (What item 5) but does not specify whether this deletion is implemented inside `bind_confirm` itself or inline in the new consumer branch. If it modifies `bind_confirm` directly, that changes behavior for all existing callers of bind_confirm that do NOT involve a hard-delete re-create, and the plan provides no acknowledgement of those callers, no expand-contract sequencing, and no rollback path for the binding-store state change.
findings.location: What item 5; Scope section ('applier.py ... bind_confirm')
findings.suggested_fix: If the deletion is local to the consumer branch (recommended): reword What item 5 to 'after bind_confirm(local_id, new_key) returns, explicitly delete reverse[old_key] from the binding store in the same transaction/save — this cleanup is only in the create_after_hard_delete consumer branch, not in bind_confirm itself.' This makes it safe and requires no callsite audit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify Step 5's implementation: specify whether the stale-reverse cleanup requires a new method on `BindingStore` (add `binding_store.py` to Scope) or is achievable via the existing `unbind()` + `bind_pending()` + `bind_confirm()` call sequence in `applier.py`, and confirm the atomicity mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section lists `apply_inbound.py`, `applier.py`, `dispatch_one.py` (read-only via existing `create_one`), and `tests` — but omits `binding_store.py`. Step 5 mandates that `reverse[old_key]` be explicitly deleted 'in the same save' as `bind_confirm`. `bind_confirm` (binding_store.py:277-289) does NOT delete stale reverse entries for the old key — it only adds `reverse[new_key]`. Whether Step 5 requires a new method on `BindingStore` or a call sequence in `applier.py` using the existing `unbind()` first, the plan leaves the implementation ambiguous and omits `binding_store.py` from scope if a new helper is needed.
findings.location: What section item 5 / Scope section; binding_store.py:277-289

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in What #3 or #4 the exact fallback when `create_one` returns `None` (budget-deferred) from the follow-on consumer branch — e.g. 'pass a `deferred_creates` list and re-enqueue for the next pass' or 'treat None return as a per-mutation failure and log it' — so the implementer knows what to code.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the fallback when `create_one` returns `None` due to the REST-call budget guard (i.e. `rest_calls >= _REST_CALL_BUDGET`). In the normal outbound batch path, budget-deferred mutations are appended to `deferred_creates`; the plan's What #3 calls `create_one` directly from the applier follow-on branch but names no `deferred_creates` list or equivalent carry-forward mechanism for this path.
findings.location: What #3 ('bind_pending(local_id) then routes through dispatch_one.create_one'); dispatch_one.py:188-192

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a What-section item describing the end-to-end test for AC6: specify the harness (e.g. in-process integration test with mocked Jira API), the trigger (synthetic inbound hard-delete event), and what 'one pass' means (single reconciler loop call), so AC6 is implementable without guesswork.
findings.finding: AC6 ('End-to-end test: inbound hard-delete -> outbound re-create in one pass') has no corresponding described work item in the What section. What items 1–8 describe unit/integration tests for specific sub-behaviors (idempotency, guard, stale-binding), but none describes an end-to-end test spanning the full inbound->outbound path. AC6 is an orphan criterion with no defined scope or implementation guidance.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6; What section items 1–8
findings.suggested_fix: Append a What item 9: 'End-to-end integration test: construct a synthetic inbound hard-delete reconciler event (mocked Jira API, real apply_inbound + applier pipeline), run one reconciler pass, assert outbound create_one was called with correct fields, local_id is bound to the new key, and reverse[old_key] is absent. This test is distinct from unit tests in items 4/6 in that it exercises the full pipeline rather than individual functions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why the re-create must be dispatched via direct mid-loop `create_one` rather than appending a typed `Mutation(outbound, create, ...)` to the outbound list (which respects `abort_check`, the `persist` flag, and the established typed-dispatch machinery); if the mid-loop call is retained, add an explicit `abort_check()` call immediately before `create_one` and a `persist` gate.
findings.finding: The plan routes the hard-delete re-create through a direct mid-inbound-loop `dispatch_one.create_one` call, bypassing the typed dispatch table (`_LEAVES`/`_apply_typed`) and — critically — the `abort_check` lease guard that wraps every other mutation in the inbound loop. A process that loses its ref-lock lease between mutations will abort subsequent inbound mutations but will have already issued the `create_one` Jira API call without any lease check. The plan does not justify this bypass vs. the established alternative of appending a typed `Mutation(outbound, create, ...)` to the outbound list and letting it flow through the normal outbound path (which is the existing typed-dispatch pattern for outbound creates).
findings.location: What section, item 3: 'Consumer wiring: in applier.py:469-471, add a branch for action=="create_after_hard_delete" that bind_pending(local_id) then routes through dispatch_one.create_one'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a startup migration step (or a `recover_pending_bindings`-style scan) that removes `reverse` entries pointing to keys confirmed as hard-deleted, so stale entries from pre-fix reconciler passes are cleaned from persisted stores without requiring a re-create to trigger.
findings.finding: The plan's What #5 / AC item 4 changes `bind_confirm` to delete `reverse[old_key]` on re-create, but specifies no startup migration or scan to clean pre-existing stale `reverse[old_key]` entries in stores already written by the old (buggy) code. Deployments where the reconciler previously ran the broken path (follow-on built but never consumed) will have orphaned reverse entries that the new bind_confirm path never touches, because bind_confirm only fires when a re-create actually executes.
findings.location: What #5 / AC item 4: 'After re-create, local_id bound to NEW key AND stale reverse[old_key] removed in the same save'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or cite the existing location of) a formal vocabulary for the `reason` key's valid values (`hard_delete`, `redirect`, `out_of_window`, `trash`) co-located with `_apply_inbound_delete` — e.g. as a `Literal` type, an `Enum`, or a `frozenset` constant — so the canonical set is a single source of truth.
findings.finding: The `reason` key's value vocabulary is referenced but never formally defined as a schema or enum. The plan declares 'CANONICAL = `reason`' and mentions four branch values (hard_delete, redirect, out_of_window, trash) scattered across What #1 and #6, but no co-located enum definition or schema is specified or cited — an implementer must infer the valid set from multiple locations.
findings.location: What #1 ('CANONICAL = `reason`') and What #6 ('redirect/out_of_window/trash do NOT')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_INBOUND_DELETE_REASONS = frozenset({'hard_delete', 'redirect', 'out_of_window', 'trash'})` constant at the top of `apply_inbound.py` alongside `_apply_inbound_delete`, and reference it from the docstring and the guard test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `old_jira_key` field to the follow-on dict built at `apply_inbound.py:553-558` (set to the canonical Jira key, e.g. the `target` when it is already a Jira key, or derived via `_local_id_to_jira_key`), and reference it by that name in What #5's stale-reverse-cleanup instruction.
findings.finding: The stale-binding cleanup in What #5 requires deleting `reverse[old_key]`, but `old_key` (the hard-deleted Jira key) is not explicitly propagated into the follow-on dict as a named field. The follow-on carries `target` (ambiguously the original target of the inbound delete mutation) but the plan never confirms that `target` equals `old_key` or specifies the field name under which the consumer should read it.
findings.location: What #5 ('Explicitly delete the stale `reverse[old_key]`'); apply_inbound.py:553-558
findings.suggested_fix: In the follow-on dict, add `"old_jira_key": target` (after verifying `target` is always a Jira key at this call site) so the consumer branch in applier.py can unambiguously do `reverse.pop(follow_on['old_jira_key'], None)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly where `old_key` originates in the consumer branch (e.g., 'retrieved from `bindings[local_id]` before `bind_pending` overwrites it, OR carried in the follow-on payload as `deleted_key`') so the stale-`reverse` cleanup in step 5 is unambiguous.
findings.finding: Step 5 requires deleting `reverse[old_key]` in the same save, but the plan never states that `old_key` (the deleted Jira key) is present in the existing `action="create_after_hard_delete"` follow-on payload. The payload is described only as carrying `fields` (title/description/priority/status/assignee, labels). If `old_key` is absent from the payload, the executor cannot implement the stale-binding cleanup without investigating and potentially modifying the payload shape — a real decision deferred to the executor.
findings.location: What section, step 5; AC bullet 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to step 5: 'Read `old_key = bindings.get(local_id)` from the binding store BEFORE calling `bind_pending`; use it to delete `reverse[old_key]` in the same save.' This avoids widening the follow-on payload shape and is self-contained.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the atomicity mechanism for the combined bind_confirm + stale-reverse-delete operation (e.g., single dict mutation committed in one flush, or a mini-WAL) and require a test that simulates a crash between the two steps to confirm the store is either fully updated or rolls back cleanly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that bind_confirm and the stale reverse[old_key] deletion happen 'in the same save', but does not specify the atomicity mechanism (single dict flush, transaction, write-ahead log, etc.). A crash or exception between the bind_confirm write and the reverse-entry deletion leaves the bindings store in a corrupt state: local_id is bound to new_key but reverse still maps old_key to local_id, causing ghost dedup hits on future passes.
findings.location: What item 5 / AC item 4 (stale-binding lifecycle)
findings.suggested_fix: Encapsulate both mutations (set bindings[local_id]=new_key, set reverse[new_key]=local_id, del reverse[old_key]) in a single function that writes the whole bindings dict atomically (e.g., serialize and fsync a single JSON file, or use a SQLite transaction). Require the AC to name this mechanism explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback schema state assertion to the plan: describe what the binding store looks like after rollback and confirm old-code reads of that state are safe (or prescribe a compensating-forward procedure with a DATA LOSS RISK note if reversibility is ambiguous).
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the state of the persisted binding store AFTER a code rollback. What #5 changes bind_confirm to delete `reverse[old_key]` atomically (via `save()`). If the new code runs bind_confirm once and then the code is rolled back, the store is left without `reverse[old_key]` — the post-rollback schema state is unspecified and potentially inconsistent with old-code assumptions about what reverse entries exist.
findings.location: What #5: 'Explicitly delete the stale reverse[old_key] ... in the same save'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC 6 whether the E2E test runs against a live/sandbox Jira instance or a mock, and if mocked, document the rationale (e.g., 'live Jira exercised in the existing create_one test suite; this test covers the dispatch wiring only') so the E6(f) gap is explicitly acknowledged.
findings.finding: AC bullet 6 requires an 'end-to-end test: inbound hard-delete -> outbound re-create in one pass,' but the plan does not specify whether this test exercises the live Jira API (or an approved sandbox) vs. a fully mocked HTTP layer. Under E6(f), `create_one` has a live external boundary (Jira REST API); a mocked E2E that stubs out the HTTP call bypasses the new consumer branch's riskiest behavior (actual issue creation + key binding) and cannot prove the cutover path.
findings.location: AC bullet 6; What section step 3 (create_one route)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC bullet (e.g., `pytest tests/test_apply_inbound.py::test_hard_delete_reason_key` or `grep -R 'create_after_hard_delete' applier.py`) so each criterion produces evidence on success without executor inference.
findings.finding: No AC provides a concrete proving command (e.g., a specific `pytest` invocation, a `grep` anchor, or a `git log` check) for verifying any individual criterion. Every AC uses passive constructions ('tests', 'test asserting', 'logged') with no path or command that would produce evidence on success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 6 bullets)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step (in Scope/AC) to update ADR 0027/0028 (or add a binding-lifecycle addendum) documenting the new invariant: bind_confirm must also delete reverse[old_key] when rebinding a local_id to a new Jira key.
findings.finding: The stale-binding lifecycle correction in item 5 (explicitly deleting reverse[old_key] and any stale bindings[local_id]==old_key in the same save) amends the binding contract that ADRs 0027/0028 describe, but the plan does not mention updating those ADRs to reflect the new invariant. Consumers of the ADRs will see a stale lifecycle description after this change ships.
findings.location: What item 5 / Acceptance Criteria item 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'ADR 0027 (or 0028) binding-lifecycle section: document that on re-create, the stale reverse[old_key] entry is deleted in the same save as bind_confirm.' Add a corresponding AC bullet: '- [ ] ADR 0027/0028 binding-lifecycle section updated to reflect reverse[old_key] deletion on rebind.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and test the retirement path for a bind_pending entry when create_one fails permanently (non-retriable error): either a max-retry count after which the pending entry is removed and the failure is escalated, or an explicit dead-letter/alert mechanism, and add an AC line covering this case.
findings.finding: The plan addresses transient create_one failures (5xx/timeout/budget) via per-mutation retry, but does not define a retirement/cleanup path for the pending binding written by bind_pending when create_one fails permanently (e.g., 4xx bad-fields or authorization error). The pending entry accumulates in the bindings store indefinitely with no expiry, GC, or dead-letter path specified.
findings.location: What item 4 (idempotency) / What item 5 (stale-binding lifecycle) / Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC6: specify whether the end-to-end test exercises the live Jira API boundary (or a recorded/contract equivalent), or explicitly name a child/follow-on ticket where live integration coverage is added — a purely offline mock satisfying AC6 leaves the real create_one→Jira path unvalidated.
findings.finding: AC6's end-to-end test ('inbound hard-delete -> outbound re-create in one pass') does not specify whether it exercises the live Jira API boundary or runs entirely offline. Because `create_one` in `dispatch_one.py` calls the real Jira API, an offline-only mock test can satisfy AC6 while leaving the live code path untested — a proxy-validation gap on the newly-enabled create path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6: 'End-to-end test: inbound hard-delete -> outbound re-create in one pass.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC6: 'End-to-end test exercises create_one against a recorded Jira response (VCR cassette) or a live integration environment; a mock-only test does not satisfy this criterion.' Alternatively, add a separate AC: 'A live-or-VCR integration test confirms the Jira create API is called with the correct payload shape.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the consumer branch spec (What item 3) that `create_one` is called with the same `client` already resolved for the inbound loop, and specify the intended behavior when `client is None` (read-only mode — should the follow-on be deferred or skipped?).
findings.finding: `applier.py:469-471` (the follow-on consumer loop) only processes follow-ons from the `inbound_typed` loop — it does NOT handle follow-ons from the outbound or untyped batch paths. The plan's new `action=='create_after_hard_delete'` branch is added at line 470 inside this loop. However, `create_one` (the target of the new branch) requires a real `client` that can issue REST calls, but within the inbound dispatch loop `client` is only resolved when `inbound_typed` is non-empty and the mode is not read-only. The plan does not address whether the same `client` instance is passed to `create_one` in the consumer branch, or whether a separate client resolution is needed.
findings.location: What section item 3; applier.py:440-476

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to Step 3/4 an explicit statement of when `binding_store.save()` is called after `bind_pending` in the consumer branch (either immediately before `create_one` or immediately after), and confirm the write-ahead protocol is durable before the Jira API call.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify WHEN `binding_store.save()` is called after `bind_pending` + (the `bind_confirm` inside `create_one`) execute inside the inbound loop. If no explicit save occurs before the outbound batch, a crash between those calls leaves a stale `pending` entry that survives to the next pass — which is the correct recovery path — but the plan's Step 4 claims idempotency is handled without explaining how the binding state is persisted after the consumer branch runs.
findings.location: What / Step 4 (Idempotency); What / Step 3 (Consumer wiring at applier.py:469-471)
findings.suggested_fix: Call `binding_store.save()` immediately after `bind_pending(local_id)` and before `create_one(...)` in the consumer branch, mirroring the write-ahead protocol documented in binding_store.py:7-16. The plan should name this call explicitly.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
